core_concepts.title: Homeostasis & Feedback
core_concepts.summary: Dynamic equilibrium maintained by negative feedback loops
core_concepts.content: The body regulates temperature, glucose, fluid, and pH through sensors, control centers, and effectors. Disease begins when feedback fails or set points shift.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Nursing supports compensatory mechanisms until treatment restores balance.

core_concepts.title: Inflammation & Immune Response
core_concepts.summary: Vascular and cellular phases — cardinal signs
core_concepts.content: Inflammation is protective response to injury/infection. Sepsis-3 defines sepsis as infection with organ dysfunction — not SIRS alone.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Fever, tachycardia, and hypotension may signal sepsis — hour-1 bundle.

core_concepts.title: Fluid & Electrolyte Balance
core_concepts.summary: Intravascular vs interstitial vs intracellular compartments
core_concepts.content: Starling forces and RAAS regulate volume. Third-spacing in burns, sepsis, and pancreatitis reduces effective circulating volume.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Daily weights and I&O detect fluid shifts before hypotension.
nursing_implications: Match fluid type to deficit type

core_concepts.title: Electrolyte Balance
core_concepts.summary: Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ roles in nerve, muscle, and cardiac function
core_concepts.content: Hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity risk. Hyperkalemia causes peaked T waves progressing to sine wave — never IV K⁺ push.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: ECG changes precede cardiac arrest in severe K⁺ abnormalities.
nursing_implications: Replace K⁺ orally/IV drip per order only

core_concepts.title: Acid-Base & ABG Interpretation
core_concepts.summary: pH, PaCO₂, HCO₃⁻ — respiratory vs metabolic disorders
core_concepts.content: Interpret: pH → primary disorder (PaCO₂ vs HCO₃⁻) → compensation → anion gap if metabolic acidosis (MUDPILES). COPD may have compensated chronic respiratory acidosis baseline.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Kussmaul respirations in metabolic acidosis; COPD retain CO₂ — titrate O₂.
nursing_implications: Report acute changes in mental status with ABG shifts

core_concepts.title: Compensation & Decompensation
core_concepts.summary: RAAS, SNS, and respiratory compensation maintain perfusion until failure
core_concepts.content: Heart failure compensation: RAAS activation, vasoconstriction, fluid retention — eventually pulmonary edema. Septic shock: warm phase then cold shock with decompensation.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Daily weights in CHF; lactate in sepsis.

core_concepts.title: Clotting & Hemostasis
core_concepts.summary: Primary platelet plug and secondary coagulation cascade
core_concepts.content: Anticoagulants block cascade steps. HIT: thrombocytopenia with thrombosis despite low platelets — stop heparin.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Bleeding vs clotting risk with anticoagulation.

core_concepts.title: Innate vs Adaptive Immunity
core_concepts.content: Innate is immediate; adaptive provides memory (vaccines). Neutropenia requires protective precautions.
nursing_implications: Avoid fresh flowers/raw foods when neutropenic

core_concepts.title: Oxygenation & Perfusion
core_concepts.summary: O₂ delivery = CO × CaO₂; utilization at cellular level
core_concepts.content: Hypoxemia from V/Q mismatch, shunt, hypoventilation, or diffusion defect. Anemia reduces O₂ carrying capacity despite normal SpO₂.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: SpO₂ does not replace hemoglobin assessment in hemorrhage.

core_concepts.title: Pain Pathophysiology
core_concepts.content: Acute pain warns of injury; chronic pain involves maladaptive CNS changes. Opioids modulate perception — respiratory depression is priority adverse effect.
core_concepts.clinical_relevance: Assess RR before each opioid dose.

disease_processes.name: Heart Failure
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Pump failure → RAAS activation → sodium/water retention → increased preload → pulmonary congestion (left) or systemic edema/JVD (right).

disease_processes.name: COPD
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Chronic inflammation and airflow limitation → air trapping → chronic hypercapnia with compensated respiratory acidosis baseline.
nursing_priorities: Avoid high-flow O₂ if CO₂ retainer

disease_processes.name: Pneumonia
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Alveolar consolidation impairs gas exchange → hypoxemia, fever, leukocytosis.
nursing_priorities: Reposition q2h — affected-side-up only for abscess/effusion per order

disease_processes.name: Hypovolemic Shock
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Volume loss → decreased preload → decreased CO → compensatory tachycardia then hypotension and organ hypoperfusion.

disease_processes.name: Cardiogenic Shock
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Pump failure → decreased forward flow → pulmonary congestion and hypotension despite adequate volume.

disease_processes.name: Sepsis / Septic Shock
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Dysregulated host response to infection → vasodilation, capillary leak, microthrombi → distributive shock and organ dysfunction.
nursing_priorities: Hour-1 bundle: cultures, antibiotics, fluids, lactate

disease_processes.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Insulin deficiency → hyperglycemia, lipolysis, ketogenesis → metabolic acidosis. Total body K⁺ depleted despite serum K⁺ may be normal/high.
nursing_priorities: Insulin infusion per protocol after K⁺ ≥3.3
Monitor glucose and K⁺ hourly initially

disease_processes.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency → chronic hyperglycemia → microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and macrovascular complications.
manifestations: HHNS if severe hyperglycemia without ketosis

disease_processes.name: Acute & Chronic Renal Failure
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Prerenal (hypoperfusion), intrarenal (ATN), postrenal (obstruction) → decreased GFR → fluid overload, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, uremia.

disease_processes.name: ARDS
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Diffuse alveolar damage → increased permeability → non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema → refractory hypoxemia. Berlin: P/F <300 with PEEP ≥5 and bilateral infiltrates.

disease_processes.name: Stroke (CVA)
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Ischemic (thrombus/embolus) or hemorrhagic rupture → focal deficit by vascular territory. Penumbra is salvageable tissue in ischemic stroke.

disease_processes.name: Asthma Exacerbation
disease_processes.pathophysiology: Bronchospasm, inflammation, mucus plugging → increased airway resistance and air trapping.

compare_contrast_pairs.title: Hypovolemic vs Cardiogenic Shock
key_differences: Fluid bolus helps vs may worsen
clinical_pearls: Assess JVD and lung sounds before aggressive fluids
Cardiogenic needs inotropes not large fluid bolus

compare_contrast_pairs.title: DKA vs HHNS
key_differences: Ketones and acidosis present in DKA
HHNS: extreme hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, minimal ketones
DKA more common type 1; HHNS type 2
clinical_pearls: Both need fluids and insulin protocol
K⁺ replacement before insulin if low

compare_contrast_pairs.title: Prerenal vs Intrarenal AKI
key_differences: Urine Na <20 prerenal; muddy brown casts intrarenal
Responds to fluids vs may need dialysis
clinical_pearls: Diuretics and GI bleed confound BUN:Cr

compare_contrast_pairs.title: Left-Sided vs Right-Sided Heart Failure
clinical_pearls: Position: high Fowler's for pulmonary edema

compare_contrast_pairs.title: Ischemic vs Hemorrhagic Stroke
key_differences: Thrombolytics for ischemic if within window
clinical_pearls: NPO and swallow eval for both

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Failure Post-Arrest
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Pump failure → intracellular Na⁺ and K⁺ leak → depolarization
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: Continuous cardiac monitoring; K⁺ replacement per lab and protocol — never IV push
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Which ECG change appears first with rising serum K⁺?
what_breaks_down_scenarios.explanation: Peaked T waves are early hyperkalemia sign.
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Hyperkalemia depolarizes cardiac cells — peaked T waves are earliest tele sign before lethal arrhythmias.

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: Insulin Deficiency in DKA
what_breaks_down_scenarios.normal_function: Insulin drives glucose into cells and suppresses ketogenesis
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Lipolysis → free fatty acids → ketone bodies → metabolic acidosis
clinical_signs: K⁺ may appear normal despite depletion
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: Fluids, insulin per protocol after K⁺ ≥3.3 mEq/L
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Why may serum K⁺ be normal/high in DKA despite total body depletion?
options: H+/K+ shift out of cells in acidosis
what_breaks_down_scenarios.explanation: Acidosis shifts K⁺ extracellularly — drops with insulin treatment.
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Acidosis drives H⁺ into cells exchanging K⁺ out — serum K⁺ falsely normal until insulin and correction of acidosis.

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: Capillary Leak in Septic Shock
what_breaks_down_scenarios.normal_function: Intact endothelium retains fluid and protein in vasculature
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Cytokine-mediated leak → third-spacing and relative hypovolemia
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: Early antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors for MAP ≥65
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Septic shock is classified as which shock type?
what_breaks_down_scenarios.explanation: Vasodilation and maldistribution define distributive shock.
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Septic shock is distributive — fluid leaks to interstitium despite adequate volume resuscitation.

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: RAAS Activation in CHF
what_breaks_down_scenarios.normal_function: RAAS maintains BP through sodium and water retention
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Compensation becomes maladaptive → volume overload → pulmonary edema
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: Daily weights, diuretics, sodium restriction, notify for acute dyspnea
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Which daily finding best tracks fluid status in CHF?
what_breaks_down_scenarios.explanation: 2–3 lb gain in days signals fluid retention.
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Daily weight detects 1–2 L fluid gain before pulmonary symptoms — cornerstone of CHF self-management teaching.

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: CO₂ Retention in COPD
what_breaks_down_scenarios.normal_function: Hypoxic drive maintains ventilation in chronic CO₂ retainers
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Over-oxygenation may suppress drive → rising PaCO₂ and acidosis
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: Titrate O₂ to SpO₂ 88–92%; ABG monitoring
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Target SpO₂ for COPD exacerbation is typically:
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Titrate O₂ to SpO₂ 88–92% — prevents CO₂ narcosis from blunted hypoxic ventilatory drive.

what_breaks_down_scenarios.title: Prerenal AKI from Dehydration
what_breaks_down_scenarios.breakdown: Hypoperfusion → GFR drop → BUN rises disproportionately
what_breaks_down_scenarios.nursing_response: IV fluids per order; strict I&O; hold nephrotoxic drugs
what_breaks_down_scenarios.question: Prerenal AKI often improves with:
what_breaks_down_scenarios.explanation: Restore renal perfusion if intrinsic kidney intact.
what_breaks_down_scenarios.clinical_why: Prerenal AKI reverses with perfusion restoration — BUN:Cr >20:1 and concentrated urine support diagnosis.

flashcards.clinical_why: Pulmonary congestion — crackles, orthopnea, pink frothy sputum

flashcards.back: Peripheral edema, JVD, hepatomegaly
flashcards.clinical_why: Peripheral edema, JVD, hepatomegaly

flashcards.back: Flat JVD early, tachycardia, responds to fluid bolus
flashcards.clinical_why: Flat JVD early, tachycardia, responds to fluid bolus

flashcards.back: JVD, crackles, poor forward flow — fluids cautious
flashcards.clinical_why: JVD, crackles, poor forward flow — fluids cautious

flashcards.back: Distributive — warm then cold; MAP ≥65 goal
flashcards.clinical_why: Distributive — warm then cold; MAP ≥65 goal

flashcards.clinical_why: Hyperglycemia + ketones + metabolic acidosis

flashcards.clinical_why: SpO₂ 88–92%; monitor ABG baseline — avoid over-O₂

flashcards.clinical_why: P/F <300 with PEEP ≥5 and bilateral infiltrates

flashcards.clinical_why: BUN:Cr >20:1, urine Na <20, responds to fluids

flashcards.back: Uteroplacental insufficiency — stop oxytocin, resuscitation
flashcards.clinical_why: Uteroplacental insufficiency — stop oxytocin, resuscitation

flashcards.back: Hold insulin if K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L until replaced
flashcards.clinical_why: Hold insulin if K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L until replaced

flashcards.back: Last known well; CT before thrombolytics; NPO
flashcards.clinical_why: Last known well; CT before thrombolytics; NPO

flashcards.back: Low platelets + thrombosis on heparin — stop heparin
flashcards.clinical_why: Low platelets + thrombosis on heparin — stop heparin

flashcards.back: Kussmaul respirations; anion gap if MUDPILES
flashcards.clinical_why: Kussmaul respirations; anion gap if MUDPILES

flashcards.back: Peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave
flashcards.clinical_why: Peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave

flashcards.back: U waves, flat T, digoxin toxicity risk
flashcards.clinical_why: U waves, flat T, digoxin toxicity risk

flashcards.back: Extreme hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, minimal ketones
flashcards.clinical_why: Extreme hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, minimal ketones

flashcards.back: Alveolar consolidation — O₂, antibiotics, IS
flashcards.clinical_why: Alveolar consolidation — O₂, antibiotics, IS

flashcards.back: Low tidal volume; prone positioning if ordered
flashcards.clinical_why: Low tidal volume; prone positioning if ordered

flashcards.back: qSOFA: RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100
flashcards.clinical_why: qSOFA: RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100

flashcards.back: RAAS retains Na/H₂O in low CO states
flashcards.clinical_why: RAAS retains Na/H₂O in low CO states

flashcards.back: Muddy brown casts; may need dialysis
flashcards.clinical_why: Muddy brown casts; may need dialysis

flashcards.back: Screening tool — not sole sepsis diagnosis
flashcards.clinical_why: Screening tool — not sole sepsis diagnosis

flashcards.back: Impending respiratory failure — not improvement
flashcards.clinical_why: Impending respiratory failure — not improvement

flashcards.clinical_why: Reduced CaO₂ despite normal SpO₂ possible

practice_questions.question: Which shock type presents with warm flushed skin early then hypotension?
practice_questions.explanation: Septic shock begins with vasodilation and warm periphery; cold shock and hypotension follow as compensation fails.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Early recognition drives sepsis hour-1 bundle: cultures, antibiotics, fluids, lactate.

practice_questions.question: JVD and bilateral crackles suggest:
practice_questions.explanation: JVD reflects elevated CVP; crackles indicate pulmonary edema from left-sided backup — diurese cautiously and monitor output.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Giving rapid fluids worsens pulmonary edema in cardiogenic shock.

practice_questions.question: First intervention in DKA after IV access:
practice_questions.explanation: DKA causes profound dehydration from osmotic diuresis — isotonic fluids restore perfusion before insulin lowers glucose and shifts K⁺.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Fluids reduce mortality; insulin without volume resuscitation worsens hypotension.

practice_questions.question: Hold insulin in DKA when K⁺ is:
practice_questions.explanation: Insulin drives K⁺ into cells — starting insulin when K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L can cause fatal arrhythmia.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Total body K⁺ is depleted in DKA despite normal/high serum levels.

practice_questions.question: COPD chronic baseline may include:
practice_questions.explanation: Chronic CO₂ retention leads kidneys to retain HCO₃⁻ compensating metabolic component — pH may be near normal.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Acute O₂ overdose can unmask uncompensated respiratory acidosis.

practice_questions.question: Refractory hypotension in septic shock after fluids — next:
practice_questions.explanation: After 30 mL/kg crystalloid, persistent hypotension needs vasopressors — norepinephrine first-line for septic shock per Surviving Sepsis.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Delaying vasopressors prolongs organ hypoperfusion and lactate elevation.

practice_questions.question: Prerenal AKI BUN:Cr ratio typically:
practice_questions.explanation: Prerenal azotemia from hypoperfusion concentrates BUN disproportionately — improves with fluid resuscitation.
practice_questions.clinical_why: BUN:Cr >20:1 with low urine Na⁺ supports prerenal cause before starting nephrotoxins.

practice_questions.question: Before thrombolytics in suspected stroke:
options: CT head to rule out hemorrhage
practice_questions.explanation: Non-contrast CT head rules out hemorrhage before thrombolytics — IV tPA contraindicated in bleed.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Time from symptom onset determines tPA eligibility — CT must not delay unnecessarily.

practice_questions.question: Hyperkalemia ECG progression includes:
practice_questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia ECG progression: peaked T → flattened P → wide QRS → sine wave → VF/asystole.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Calcium stabilizes membrane while K⁺ lowering therapies take effect.

practice_questions.question: Daily weight gain of 2 lb in CHF signals:
practice_questions.explanation: 2–3 lb weight gain in 24–48 hr equals ~1–1.5 L fluid retention — notify before crackles and orthopnea worsen.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Daily weights are more sensitive than single lung auscultation for early CHF decompensation.

practice_questions.question: DKA vs HHNS: ketones are prominent in:
practice_questions.explanation: DKA (type 1): insulin deficiency → ketogenesis and metabolic acidosis. HHNS (type 2): profound hyperglycemia >600 mg/dL with osmotic diuresis but minimal ketones.
practice_questions.clinical_why: DKA needs insulin urgently after fluids and K⁺ check; HHNS needs aggressive fluids first — both are emergencies.

practice_questions.question: ARDS P/F ratio Berlin definition uses PEEP:
practice_questions.explanation: Berlin ARDS definition: P/F ratio <300 with bilateral infiltrates not fully explained by cardiac failure, on PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O within 7 days of insult.
practice_questions.clinical_why: ARDS management is lung-protective ventilation — low tidal volume 6 mL/kg IBW and plateau pressure <30.

practice_questions.question: HIT on heparin — priority action:
practice_questions.explanation: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT): platelets drop 30–50% on heparin with paradoxical thrombosis — stop all heparin including flushes, start alternative anticoagulant.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Continuing heparin in HIT causes limb loss and PE — 4T score guides suspicion before antibody testing.

practice_questions.question: Left-sided HF classic finding:
practice_questions.explanation: Left ventricular failure backs blood into pulmonary circulation — crackles, S₃, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, pink frothy sputum in acute pulmonary edema.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Right-sided failure shows JVD and peripheral edema — many patients have biventricular failure.

practice_questions.question: Metabolic acidosis compensation:
practice_questions.explanation: Metabolic acidosis triggers compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul respirations) to blow off CO₂ and raise pH — check ABG for pH, PaCO₂, HCO₃⁻.
practice_questions.clinical_why: DKA and sepsis cause metabolic acidosis — treat underlying cause, not only compensation.

practice_questions.question: Sepsis-3 definition emphasizes:
practice_questions.explanation: Sepsis-3 defines sepsis as suspected infection plus acute organ dysfunction (SOFA/qSOFA score change) — SIRS alone is insufficient.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad antibiotics, fluids 30 mL/kg if hypotensive.

practice_questions.question: Stroke penumbra is:
practice_questions.explanation: Ischemic penumbra surrounds infarct core — hypoperfused but viable tissue salvageable with reperfusion (tPA, thrombectomy) within time windows.
practice_questions.clinical_why: Every 15-minute delay in stroke treatment worsens disability — 'time is brain.'

practice_questions.question: K⁺ 6.8 mEq/L — priority nursing action:
options: Continuous cardiac monitoring and notify provider
practice_questions.explanation: K⁺ 6.8 mEq/L is life-threatening — continuous tele, notify provider, prepare calcium gluconate for ECG changes; never IV K⁺ push.
practice_questions.clinical_why: ECG changes precede arrest — peaked T, widened QRS, sine wave pattern requires immediate treatment.
